question: Define computational linguistics and explain its primary goal.
expectedPoints: Computational linguistics is an interdisciplinary field that combines linguistics and computer science.
Its primary goal is to develop computational models that can process, understand, and generate human language.
It serves as the foundational science for applied fields like Natural Language Processing (NLP).

question: Compare and contrast a context-free grammar (CFG) with a probabilistic context-free grammar (PCFG). Explain one advantage of using a PCFG for syntactic parsing.
expectedPoints: A CFG consists of a set of rules (productions) that define the syntax of a language without probabilities.
A PCFG augments a CFG by assigning a probability to each rule, representing how likely that rule is to be used.
An advantage of a PCFG is its ability to rank competing parses for an ambiguous sentence; the parse with the higher probability (product of rule probabilities) is preferred.

question: Describe the fundamental architecture of a transformer model, focusing on the self-attention mechanism. Explain how self-attention allows the model to overcome limitations of previous architectures like RNNs.
expectedPoints: The core architecture is built on an encoder-decoder structure, though models like BERT (encoder-only) and GPT (decoder-only) use variations.

The self-attention mechanism computes a weighted sum of all words in the input sequence for each word, where the weights (attention scores) represent the relevance of every other word to the current one.

It overcomes RNN limitations by allowing parallel computation of all positions simultaneously (faster training) and by directly modeling long-range dependencies without the vanishing gradient problem, as any two words can interact regardless of distance.
